title: Summer hive inspection, step by step
subtitle: What to look for each time you open the hive.
excerpt: A summer inspection takes around 20 minutes on a calm colony and covers the same checklist every time: queen presence, brood health, space, disease signs, and swarm preparations. Getting the routine consistent means you catch problems early rather than discovering them in a panic.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. BBKA Basic Assessment syllabus (2023), inspection methodology. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive; foundational inspection sequence still relevant for the brood-reading sections.

glossaryTerms.definition: Eggs, larvae, and pupae inside comb cells. Healthy brood has a characteristic smell of warm bread; diseased brood smells sour or rotten.
A resinous compound collected from tree buds by bees and used to seal gaps and coat surfaces inside the hive. Sticky and brown; glues frames to each other and to the hive body.

content.text: Inspect weekly from late April through August. Choose a warm, dry, still day between 11 am and 3 pm when most foragers are out and the colony is calmest. A cold, wet, or nectar-dearth day produces a defensive colony. Both problems can usually wait a day.

method. Stand to one side of the hive, not directly in front. Give two or three puffs of cool smoke under the entrance and wait 30 seconds. Remove the roof and crown board; two or three puffs across the top of the frames sends the bees down into the box. Keep the smoker to hand but use it sparingly, over-smoking an already calm colony agitates rather than calms.
Work through the frames one by one from one side to the other. On each frame, check for:
Eggs, tiny white slivers standing upright in the base of cells. Eggs confirm the queen was present three days ago. Hold the frame so light falls into the cells.
, flat cappings, even pattern, smell of warm bread. Sunken, perforated, or slimy cappings suggest disease.
Swarm cells, peanut-shaped cells on the lower face or edges of frames. One capped cell means act today.
Space, at least two empty drawn combs in the brood box. A colony that is honey-bound (every cell full) will swarm even without swarm cells.

Solid brood pattern: cappings are slightly domed and uniform mid-brown. Very few empty cells scattered through the brood area. The colony is queenright and healthy. Shotgun pattern: many empty cells scattered through the brood, no clear laying area. Could be a failing queen, a disease outbreak (sacbrood, European foulbrood), or a colony recovering from recent stress. Look for the queen and check the eggs carefully.
Replace frames in the order you removed them. The

seal re-forms within a couple of days. Press the crown board down firmly and replace the roof. Record in your hive notebook: date, weather, queen seen (yes/no/eggs only), swarm cell status, space remaining, any disease signs. A consistent record makes the next inspection faster and helps identify trends across the season.

attrs.cue: No eggs and no queen visible
attrs.cause: Queen may have been accidentally killed at the last inspection, or she has swarmed
attrs.fix: Check the whole hive for queen cells. If there are capped cells, the colony is raising a replacement, leave them one cell and check for eggs in 14 to 18 days. If there are no cells and no eggs, introduce a frame of young eggs from another hive immediately.

attrs.cause: The colony is replacing a failing queen
attrs.fix: Find the old queen. If she is present and laying, leave the supersedure cell, the bees usually have good reasons. Check again in 10 days for a new laying queen.
